item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Never mind. You fucking got me, though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is… is the TMP really gonna crash Windows if I open it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In situations where Y2k actually created problems, they weren't just on January 1st 2000- for instance in 1997 a credit card company had issues trying to read new cards with post-2000 expiration dates.

The fix was mostly about addressing lots of little issues like that, shortcuts buried in spaghetti code from the 60s and 70s where only tracking two digits of the year meant you saved entire kilobytes of memory and hours of processing time on a large database, and timers from when a year date of 99 was just "the future".

…I… yeah, I've got nothing.
That was amazing. Good job.

item.title: The pathos here falls flat for me
item.content:encoded:
The visuals are nice and the premise was interesting, but I feel like at some point you started beating a dead horse with what was happening in the simulation. I understand that the pull of this article is supposed to be the characters and creating a sense of empathy and pathos for what they are losing, but my issue is that it feels like I was just shown like a set of people losing different types of creative endeavors without like… much expansion on it.

You have this large cast of characters, but each one feels like a flash in the pan. I don't have enough time to feel particularly attached to any of them (which I get, its flash fiction after all), but they also are all like, kind of formulaic in their introduction?

I also am not a fan of the use of the exploration team. They seem to continuously parrot apologies and sympathies without much actual agency or motive of their own. I understand that they are just supposed to be a lens for us to view the other characters from but they're also our POV, so it feels unsatisfactory to have the main group we're following throughout that log feel so flat.

Overall, I understand what this is trying to do, but it feels far too direct to have any impact for me. I can see the pathos coming from a mile away, which means that I can see how you're trying to spin the emotional bits, which blunts their impact. I wish the characters were more nuanced or the reactions had more diversity or at least induced a more meaningful change in the characters.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You said you were interested so here we go.

Firstly, I'm happy that another one of your 'gay little computers' is up, they're always a treat to read. as for the actual article, there are a few things I dislike, but I'll keep it short.

  1. The dialogue drags slightly near the middle section of the second log. I almost got to the point of having to force myself through but luckily that didn't happen.
  2. The pictures are a bid headache-inducing, but at the same time, I like how unique they are.
  3. I feel like GAW could be completely cut here and it wouldn't make a difference.

On to the actual review. The concept of a Ready Player One style digital dream world is such an obvious concept that I'm surprised hasn't been done (as far as I've read). There's a certain air of nostalgia throughout that permeates this entire read, the theme does help with that, but there's just a certain whimsicality I can't put my finger on.

As for the narrative, I'm happy you didn't make this completely focused on the digital world, because if you did I don't see this being as successful. The characters within 5470, specifically Jazzy and MIDI were my favorites. I also liked how you humanized the MTf members, it could've been very easy to make them cold but their use as foils to the people in 5470 adds another rarely-seen element. I wasn't prepared, nor did I think I was going to form a connection with these characters but the world dying was a hard-hitting moment.

I thought I would have more to say, but my feelings are pretty straightforward on this one. Great effort, great concept, and a great story. Easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One thing I don't get is the reality glitching before the year 2000. If the MacGuffin is the Y2K bug, shouldn't nothing happen before then?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it. Very emotional, capturing an experience that's such a big part of so many people's childhood and teenage years but which hasn't got a lot of coverage. It's a different use of surrealism than a lot of skips, as a way to capture and convey a real word experience that's hard to describe, and I like it. Easy +1, as anything which makes me cry should get. Glad those radical leftist pranksters of GAW took them in!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This captures how some early online communities felt really well. Happy to know that the kids ended up at Kenowhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You did an astoundingly good job capturing the melancholic feeling of when efforts to make better places for our friends falls apart and there's nothing that can be done. I cried buckets for the same sorts of goodbyes I had when MMOs or other beloved spaces disappeared

I hope some of the kids eventually went on to build Project Kenowhere, there's a lot of feelings of "making our world" in both this article and that one and like, these kids were doing this in 1997 and Kenowhere takes place in 2017, yea? somehow I'm still friends with some people from my versions of wanderer's paradise and some of them have turned into the kind of people who would make Kenowhere so maybe that's just me projecting

either way, this feels so damn real, thank you

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why, Why 2k.

Now all we need is "Revenge of the Why"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Towards the end I just had this song on replay in my head
The little addition at the end of the "checkup" on the people who were in the VR, knowing that they're okay despite all they lost, is somewhat unexpected (given the usual tone of SCP stuff) and really nice. I think I'm not the only one needing that "hope for the future" fix these days.
Anyways, +1 for sure, good work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding. I don't think this is a bad article in the slightest, but the story's presentation doesn't gel with the expectations I have for the wiki. My only other feedback is that certain parts of the description and logs — specifically with regards to describing the glitching visuals — are needlessly repetitive of what can already be discerned from the images, and doesn't need to be restated. Losing that would increase the punch the imagery has.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is some goddamn* good art. +1.

…*or is that WANdamn?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a fan of pretty much everything about this piece.

The core anomaly makes use of WAN in a fun way (and is one of the few instances I can think of where a space devoted to a religious GoI is presented as legitimately positive and uplifting), and also manages to tie in Y2K creatively - even literalizing the way many people felt at the time, that the world could end when the clock struck midnight. The images of breakdown are gorgeously trippy. The first Easter egg is a nice touch (and… more on that under the collapsible).

Most importantly, the story being told, about the importance of making a community and - in every sense - staying connected, really does hit in the way it intends to. The characters feel like part of a single whole while still having their own personalities and reactions to the impending crash. Even the reference to GAW, a GoI I personally run hot and cold about, highlights the theme of community that runs through that group as well - so the reference puts both this skip and that group in a better light, rather than cheapening either. I think ManyMeats is right that the description could probably be shaved down by a significant amount without losing anything essential - but given that the incident log is the lion's share of the piece anyway, and its length does feel earned and essential, I wasn't really bothered by any flab in the description.

Spoilers and further discussion below, for those with a dataitch
Cry glitch and call quits

In addition to the more obvious Easter egg on the external site, there are also the rectangular crossword-puzzle-looking blocks in the last three images. I think they're not quite high enough resolution as they appear in the skip, but from the original images in the Files tab, you can get what you need to decode them using the key from the control room image (20:10:54). With etoisle's permission, I'm reproducing the content below. I think they're well-written text in their own right, as well as adding interesting layers to the story that I think are a bit open to interpretation, and I'd like other people to see them too in case anyone wants to add their thoughts.

From 23:55:28:
0110 from a buggy callback i parse that i have over-stacked juniors first syphon. it implemented junior in a dimension to which it lacked protocol; it was beyond its resolution.

From 23:57:53:
0101 as it forird, nor is itce quits the s madness. thissyphon. i hear is informatiojunior call gln! unlike dataitch: this is . information weird… this.spans over mul.. this is madtiple dimensioness! /help abns. it moves uort/// in the pwards. not jumidst of the kst northwards!ludges, a figuit commits. re calmly syncs to my dcts: this is not we

From 23:59:09:
rs parse. theseare the records that document our handshakes.

First of all, I love that last line, and it really brings home the connection-in-all-senses theme of the piece. Second, the jumbled text in the middle image is intelligible as a string that loops and weaves back through itself:

As it force quits the Syphon, I hear Junior call glitch: this is weird… this is madness! /help abort. In the midst of the kludges, a figure calmly syncs to my DCTs: this is not weird, nor is it madness. This is information! Unlike data. Information spans over multiple dimensions. It moves upwards. Not just northwards! It commits.

So, third… this brings up a lot of interesting extra plot that's mentioned in the skip's top image but largely absent from the incident log. Who is Junior? Is it Jazzy? A digital entity Jazzy created, maybe unintentionally, that embodies Syphon? Xenia (probably not)? Some subroutine or facsimile of WAN (and if so, is that what WAN-DERER as a whole actually is)? And who is the figure? And of course - who is the speaker? I would presume that the speaker is WAN, and perhaps "WE" in the last line of the incident log is WAN and Junior, but that wouldn't explain who the figure is.

I guess my point in all this is: there's a whole lot here, and it gets a whole lot done - while still leaving some threads open, just in case. I'm into it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oh yeah, if id had the freedom/skill to explore something like that i definitely would have gone a little more wild with it :'D my feeling is that the "outer" spaces in the world get kind of incomprehensible to people who havent lived there, i'm inspired by things like hypnospace outlaw which treat the digital space as a kind of… uncurated, hyperpersonalized canvas for people to paint themselves onto, and behind the scenes/while it's not corrupted that's how i see the WAN-derer's paradise :'D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
oh I didn't mean to imply you made them. And it seems like visually, they fit the style that you're going for and that's to be commended.

As to the rest (and the following is not actually critique of your article because I don't think it's something you were interested in exploring here), I was thinking about how inextricable our experiences as humans are in considering what non-human cultures and constructs might be like. The idea of cultural touchstones like gardens and pavilions are used here to give importance to places and details in this article. We as the readers understand what these spaces mean, and we're given context through which to filter what we're reading.

But why should these things, like gardens or pavilions, exist at all other than that they're familiar? There's no cultural imperative for them, and I think the space of 'what social mores and spaces would emerge from a primarily digital society' is a very interesting one I'd love to see explored in more detail. But again, these two paragraphs shouldn't be construed as a critique of your article, because I don't believe that was necessarily the primary intent here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i didnt make the images. i changed it to "inconsistent topology". i'm kind of having trouble understanding everything else here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-5470 resembled a crude 3D environment, and was largely abstract and artistic in composition, often featuring noneuclidean geometry and Maxwellist iconography.

oh no, not spheres!!

But in all seriousness, "noneuclidean" as a descriptor is one of those things that Lovecraft has conditioned us to think of as haute couture, when it's really… fairly mundane. It also clashes with the images presented, which are mostly flat surfaces with weird textures.

And the images, in general, feel like they engender some degree of passivity when describing the environment. I don't think that's a bad thing, necessarily, but it feels like a very surface level take on what this sort of thing would look like. Virtual spaces pretending to be a garden, or a convention hall… all appropriately transformed, but it doesn't really challenge a humanist perspective in a way that feels meaningful. It's still a fundamentally human-oriented space, because I think the author was relying on the images to convey tone and setting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for reading! The webpage is what you see in the picture and description—no extraneous content besides the Easter egg. I explicitly didn’t want to force people to go there to understand the story.

What parts of the description felt extraneous?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's taken awhile to sum my thoughts together on this piece so I will try to wrap up what I can without wandering too far.

Overall, this has a very "YA" feel to me in presentation and narrative that I did not find appealing or personally evocative. In the interests of fairness, I can certainly recognize the amount of effort present in this scip and invested into its presentation but all the glam in the world cannot hide the words on the page. To that end I can accept that this scip just may simply not be "intended for me". If that's the case, I hope that assists you in classifying my feedback.

While there are strong human elements to the story down in the addenda, the description meanders quite a bit to the point where it's maybe…twice as long as it needs to be? In that neighborhood.

Additionally, I opened your webpage but I am not willing to investigate that as an element of storytelling. I hope that this web page is purely material that enhances what I'd find in the scip, and not that an external site is necessary to understanding your work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The presentation of this skip is phenomenal. Also love the y2k theme <3

Wholehearted +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i did make the neocities page!! i’m glad i was able to win you over :’]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly I swallowed a bit when I saw the computer lay-out in the beginning since I have seen articles that pulled it off not so great. I do think it was a great choice after seeing the corrupted/glitched images throughout the article and of course the content/context.

And now here I am emotionally invested in this page. This was beautiful written. I think this is definitely one I need to read again later. Idk if you made the linked page but props to that too. Never thought I would actually get the ZIP from it. 10/10 would download again.

Please have my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 😭🥺 thank you for taking the time to read… i'm so glad you liked it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found your easter egg in Textedit. You're very welcome, and you deserve the praise - you did excellently with this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i love that metaphor—i wanted to make something that kind of bucked the "technology bad" kneejerk reaction :']

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was unexpectedly moving. This was transhumanism with a human face - weird metaphor, but that's how I read it.

It's interesting to see the Jailers freeing people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thank you so much :']]]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All throughout reading drafts of this, I was pretty blown away by how touching this narrative is. How evocative it is of the tragedy of growing up and letting childish dreams go, so we can focus on more grounded goals…

But then, there’s that brilliant spark of hope in the crosslink at the end.

Anyway… jazzy and midi and Mx are really well realized, and I like them a lot. I hope we see them again.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Probably one of the best presented SCPs in a long while, if I am to be perfectly honest. Story is fantastic too.

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very, very, very good. You pulled this one off well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Incredible that those images are just out there as CC.

Anyways, it's a good article. Hammers in the sense of resignation in the face of the end of a world without ever sounding melodramatic or like a broken record.

Easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Read this in drafts, and still think it's excellent. I think the ending is great, and I'm glad you de-pixellated the text to the point of readability. 10/10

item.title: a dial-up haven for the troubled and angsty.
item.content:encoded:
look at my authorpage

Y2K
You let me down, now
You let me down, down
Down, down, down, down, down, down, down

thanks to Elunerazim, Grigori Karpin, ValidClay, J Dune, and Christopoulos for crit.
special thanks to daviiid for being my fwiend. :]

i wrote this mostly while under 2-week quarantine, locked in a comfortable hotel room.

i wrote the ending when i left again to breathe.

i hope you enjoy it. it feels like a combination of a lot of things i love.

another special thanks to rosa menkman for releasing so much of her beautiful art under CC-BY 2.0.

ps: there's an easter egg! :]

+ this is about what X3N14 looks like
- this is about what X3N14 looks like
